speaker: patient
text: Hi, Doctor. I’ve been feeling really unwell since yesterday—fever, fatigue, and this tightness in my chest.

speaker: doctor
text: Thanks for coming in. Let’s start by taking your vitals and reviewing your recent health status. How long has the chest tightness been going on?

speaker: doctor
text: Your systolic blood pressure today is 143 mmHg—slightly elevated but not critically so.

speaker: doctor
text: And your diastolic pressure is 72 mmHg, which is within normal range for now.

speaker: doctor
text: You’re currently on Insulin glargine 10 units once daily at night—still following that regimen?

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your history and labs, your primary diagnosis remains Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus.

speaker: doctor
text: Your fasting blood glucose this morning was 6.6 mmol/L—within target, though borderline.

speaker: doctor
text: I see you have a fever—your temperature is 39.0 °C. That’s quite high. Any chills or cough?

speaker: patient
text: Yes, the chest tightness started yesterday evening—it feels like pressure, especially when I walk up stairs.

speaker: doctor
text: Given the fever and chest symptoms, we’ll need to rule out infection or cardiac involvement. Have you had shortness of breath or palpitations?

speaker: patient
text: A little short of breath, and my heart feels like it’s racing sometimes—especially with the fever.

speaker: doctor
text: Let me recheck your blood pressure—sometimes acute illness can cause fluctuations.

speaker: doctor
text: This time, your diastolic pressure is 79 mmHg—higher than the earlier reading of 72 mmHg.

speaker: doctor
text: And your blood glucose just rose to 9.0 mmol/L—likely stress-related from the fever and inflammation.

speaker: doctor
text: Given the hyperglycemia and ongoing febrile illness, I’m going to increase your basal insulin dose to Insulin glargine 16 units once nightly.

speaker: patient
text: Okay—I’ll make sure to monitor my sugars more closely, especially with this fever.

speaker: doctor
text: Absolutely. Also, please check your temperature every 4 hours and stay well-hydrated. We’ll repeat labs tomorrow if the fever persists.

speaker: patient
text: Should I hold the insulin if I’m not eating much due to feeling sick?

speaker: doctor
text: No—don’t skip the insulin, even with reduced intake. Illness increases insulin resistance, so the higher dose is intentional. Just try small sips of water or sugar-free fluids.

speaker: patient
text: Got it. And yes—that reading of 39.0 °C is still holding; I checked again 20 minutes ago.
